name: North Lake Leelanau
description: Northern basin of Lake Leelanau; clear, deep glacial lake known for cold-water fish (e.g. trout, walleye).

name: South Lake Leelanau
description: Southern basin of Lake Leelanau; shallower and more fertile, popular for boating and fishing.

name: Little Traverse Lake
description: Small lake near Lake Michigan (Good Harbor Bay); mostly <20 ft deep with one ~55 ft hole. Offers fishing and feeds Shalda Creek.

name: Lime Lake
description: Spring-fed lake with extensive shoal areas (<15 ft); marl bottom and sparse vegetation, outlets to Little Traverse Lake.

name: Big Glen Lake
description: Large clear-blue lake in Sleeping Bear area, very deep and oligotrophic, with lake trout and cisco present.

name: Little Glen Lake
description: Shallow lagoon-like lake adjacent to Big Glen; sandy bottom, warms in summer, popular swimming spot.

name: Cedar Lake (Leelanau)
description: Long narrow lake north of Traverse City; max depth ~45–47 ft, majority shallow. Outlet to West Bay, supports mixed fishery.

name: Platte Lake (Big Platte)
description: Benzie Co. lake on Platte River; one-third is shallow shoals, deep spots ~90 ft. Salmon migrate from Lake Michigan seasonally.

name: Crystal Lake
description: Large oligotrophic lake with exceptional clarity; heavily used for sailing, fishing (lake trout) and tourism.

name: Lower Herring Lake
description: Deep lake near Lake Michigan; mostly >15 ft deep. Drains to Lake Michigan; natural shores, quiet recreation.

name: Long Lake (Grand Traverse)
description: Big all-sports lake with 5 islands; spring-fed, known for bass/pike fishing and public island parks.

name: Long Lake (Missaukee)
description: Small 60-acre lake in state forest; very shallow (max 15 ft); with rustic camping and panfish fishing.

name: Upper Herring Lake
description: Upper lake of the Herring Lakes (Benzie Co.); shallow (avg ~15 ft) and partly undeveloped. Outflows into Lower Herring Lake.

name: Lake Ann
description: Deep, clear lake near Village of Lake Ann; part of Platte River headwaters. Supports trout at depth and active summer recreation.

name: Little Platte Lake
description: Shallow  marshy lake on Platte River (Sleeping Bear Dunes NL); max ~8 ft. Rich in wildlife, canoeing and fishing for panfish.

name: Green Lake
description: Deep glacial lake by Interlochen; oligotrophic, with lake trout and annual music festivals on its shore (Interlochen Arts camp).

name: Duck Lake
description: Twin lake to Green Lake (separated by a state park); clear, supports bass/panfish and some trout. All-sports use.

name: Silver Lake
description: Kettle lake just S. of Traverse City; third deepest in GT County. Developed shores, popular for bass fishing and boating.

name: Lake Dubonnet
description: Man-made floodings (mud lake) in GT County; very shallow (~5 ft), weedy wildlife lake. Excellent for paddling and waterfowl.

name: Bear Lake (Manistee)
description: Large shallow lake in Manistee County; warm waters with abundant weeds. Good panfish and bass fishing; village of Bear Lake on east shore.

name: Portage Lake
description: Natural lake connected to Lake MI at Onekama; two deep holes (~60 ft), rest shallow. Hosts salmon from Lake MI, busy resort lake.

name: Manistee Lake
description: Lake at Manistee River mouth (harbor lake); used for shipping and fishing. Brackish mix of river and Lake MI influences, ~50 ft deep.
